title: How GPS Works: From Satellites to Your Location

slides.heading: How GPS Works
slides.body: From 20,000 km Above Earth to the Dot on Your Map

slides.heading: A Constellation in the Sky
slides.body: The GPS system uses 31 active satellites orbiting Earth at ~20,200 km altitude. They're arranged so that at least 4 satellites are visible from almost any point on the planet at any time.

slides.heading: Atomic Clocks Keep Time
slides.body: Each satellite carries 3–4 atomic clocks accurate to one second every 300 million years. This extreme precision is essential — a tiny timing error translates to hundreds of meters of location error on the ground.

slides.heading: Broadcasting Your Position
slides.body: Every satellite continuously broadcasts a radio signal containing its exact location and the precise time the signal was sent. Your GPS receiver listens for these signals traveling at the speed of light.

slides.heading: The Math of Trilateration
slides.body: Your device measures how long each signal took to arrive and multiplies by the speed of light to get distance. With 3 satellites, it narrows your position to a point; a 4th satellite eliminates clock error in the receiver.

slides.heading: Einstein's Relativity Matters
slides.body: Satellites move fast and sit in weaker gravity — both effects alter time per Einstein's theories. Without correcting for relativity, GPS positions would drift by up to 10 km per day. The system accounts for this automatically.

slides.heading: Signals Through the Atmosphere
slides.body: Radio signals slow slightly as they pass through the ionosphere and troposphere, introducing errors. Modern GPS receivers use atmospheric models and data from ground stations to correct for these delays.

slides.heading: Assisted GPS & Modern Phones
slides.body: Smartphones use A-GPS, downloading satellite almanac data over Wi-Fi or cell networks to get a fix in seconds instead of minutes. They also combine GPS with cell tower and Wi-Fi signals for accuracy indoors.

slides.heading: Beyond Navigation
slides.body: GPS powers more than maps — it synchronizes global financial networks, timestamps internet traffic, guides autonomous vehicles, enables precision farming, and coordinates disaster response in remote areas.

slides.heading: A Signal Worth Following
slides.body: Next time your phone pinpoints you instantly, remember: 31 satellites, atomic clocks, Einstein's physics, and decades of engineering are working together — all to put that dot exactly where you are.
